section_title: INTRODUCTION TO ICT PRECISION TRADING CONCEPTS
content:
This is Volume one of the ICT Precision Trading Concepts video series, part one of three. The series
focuses on specific, concise concepts for implementing price action in trading to achieve greater
consistency.
- This volume will focus on Weekly Order Blocks and Order Flow.

section_title: THE OVERLOOKED POWER OF WEEKLY ORDER BLOCKS
content:
Many traders are excited about daily and intraday Order Blocks, but you'll be surprised to find that this is
actually occurring on all timeframes. Weekly Order Blocks are often overlooked by the neophyte trader and
those that are 'technical analysts'.
- Utilizing the weekly Order Blocks and the Order Flow on a weekly basis can really direct your attention to
low risk, high probability setups.
- These setups are derived from the higher time frame perspectives, which is very important given the large
magnitude of drawdown or altogether bust that accompanies new traders.

section_title: MITIGATING UNCERTAINTY AND EMOTIONAL TRADING
content:
As speculators, we run into a lot of adversity. If you don't have a very concise plan of action from the
beginning, the emotional aspect of losing money or even gaining money can be numbing.
- A clear, concise plan of what you're looking for and why removes a lot of the uncertainty that plagues the
neophyte and new trader.
- Adopting this mindset and looking at charts with this concept in mind will lead to a whole new level of
trading.

section_title: INSTITUTIONAL TRADING ON HIGHER TIMEFRAMES
content:
Large institutions and banks base the majority of their trades from weekly and/or higher time frame
analysis charts.
- They are not looking at one and five-minute charts to move large blocks of currency; they need the
liquidity that larger Order Blocks present on higher timeframe charts.
- Their orders are so vast that they often have to be broken up into modular blocks.

section_title: SMART MONEY VS. STREET MONEY PHILOSOPHY
content:
As professional-minded traders, we look for opportunities to buy at wholesale prices and sell at retail prices.
- We want to be buying when the street money is selling and selling when the street money is buying.
- This is not achieved by pretending to be smarter or trusting math-oriented indicators, but by looking for
the tell-tale signs and fingerprints left in the charts by smart money participation in specific moves.

section_title: ICT SCOUT SNIPER FIELD GUIDE
content:
The identification of smart money fingerprints is shown in the 'Inner Circle Trader Scout Sniper Field Guide'
series.
- It is important to go through that video series first, as there will be no benefit to you without its
understanding.
- Coupling that mindset with what we're learning here will elevate your trading to a whole new level, leading
to stunning results in your understanding of the market when price should be moving.

section_title: GUARANTEED PROFITS
content:
I promise you, if you start implementing these three components to your trading and analysis, you will see
absolutely stunning results in your understanding of the market, specifically when price should be moving.
- I am not promising profits or wealth; you will still lose money trading this way.
- There will always be a certain measure of human error that will plague your trading regardless of how
concise your trading plan is.

section_title: THE FOUNDATION FOR DIRECTIONAL BIAS
content:
Trends and Order Flow on weekly charts tend to remain for a long period of time, requiring much more
significant change (e.g., fundamental level impact) to reverse direction.
- This is advantageous as it allows us, whether day traders, scalpers, short-term, swing, or position traders,
to trust our directional bias.
- There is no greater directional bias to trust your trades on than the weekly chart.

section_title: DISPELLING MYTHS ABOUT WEEKLY CHARTS
content:
The problem is that we've been lulled into believing that weekly charts are of no use to us unless we're
long-term position traders, which is absolutely not true.
- Most textbooks rarely comment on the importance or utilization of a weekly chart.
- While specific trades on that timeframe take a long time to set up, you can understand what the 'smart
money' is doing right then and now in less than two minutes.

section_title: PERSONAL JOURNEY AND THE 'THREE COMPONENTS'
content:
A personal anecdote recounts making money early in the mid-90s commodity market due to blind luck,
leading to false confidence before ultimately facing losses and needing to 'go back to school'.
- This experience highlights the importance of truly understanding market movements rather than relying
on luck.
- The concepts you will learn here helped gain a greater understanding of how markets move and how to
anticipate sizable, sustainable directional moves.
- These three components are the backbone to getting the direction right more times than not.

section_title: TRUSTING PRICE ACTION OVER FUNDAMENTAL ANALYSIS
content:
I trust my analysis because I am limited in my capacity of knowing the fundamentals behind these moves;
many of them go completely over my head.
- I don't claim to be a fundamental genius or smarter than most, but I know what makes me tick as a trader.
- I combine this with central tenets in the marketplace that are inherently generic and repeat over and over
again.
- The brilliant thing is, these concepts will not stop working just because I share them.

section_title: THE UNIVERSALITY OF MARKET TENETS AND FINANCIAL INDEPENDENCE
content:
WARNINGS
My income is derived solely from choices made on a price chart, so a clear, concise approach is essential.
- A stark warning is given to those contemplating quitting their job based on demo account performance or
small capital ($5,000).
- The paradigm shift when relying solely on trading for income creates immense uncertainty and a 'mine
scramble'; professionals are not looking to give you their money.
- Transition gradually from a job to full-time trading to build long-term success.

section_title: BUILDING LASTING SUCCESS AND RISK MANAGEMENT
content:
Having this perspective and approach to trading will stack the odds in your favor and lessen, but not
entirely remove, the likelihood of going bust.
- Good money management is crucial: never bet the farm or over-leverage, even if concepts work well
consistently.
- This is not gambling; the goal is to consistently take money out of the marketplace.
- This concept helps you know exactly what you're looking for when you sit down to charts.

section_title: FILTERING OUT NOISE
content:
Keep your sights set on what matters most, filtering out all the lower timeframes' static noise.
- A 20-pip move on a 5-minute or 15-minute chart means absolutely nothing to a longer time frame premise.
- As professionals, we use a higher timeframe perspective; we are willing to wait for the setup to come to
us, not chase tiny minor moves.
- This series is designed for those asking how to know support/resistance levels will hold, and how to
determine the daily directional premise.

section_title: MACRO PERSPECTIVE AND EMA APPLICATION
content:
Framing retail trading ideas on the basis of the macro perspective is conducive to profits.
- I don't claim to be a fundamentalist; I trust the price chart to tell me everything fundamental that I need to
know about that marketplace.
- Exponential Moving Averages (EMAs) applied to the weekly timeframes will assist in identifying higher
odds Order Flow and Order Blocks.

section_title: DEFINING WEEKLY ORDER FLOW WITH 9 & 18 EMAS
content:
Applying the 9 and 18 EMAs on the weekly chart helps define Order Flow due to the tendency for trends to
remain intact for long periods.
- For a bullish condition (weekly Order Flow up): the 9 EMA (red) is greater than the 18 EMA (blue), and
they are 'stacking' (opening up and pointing up).
- For a bearish condition (weekly Order Flow down): the 9 EMA is below the 18 EMA, and they are
'stacking' (opening up and pointing down).
- EMAs are lagging, but their slow reaction on weekly charts provides a reliable directional bias. Transition
periods and consolidations are where losses may occur.

section_title: PROFESSIONAL MINDSET AND REDUCED FEAR
content:
If you have a bullish weekly order flow and find a daily order block to buy, a 20-pip rally followed by a 10-pip
pullback shouldn't induce fear.
- You will be trading with a professional mindset, having weekly order flow, weekly order blocks, daily order
blocks, and market open/closes all in your favor.
- This greatly increases the likelihood of making money, though there's no guarantee.
- However, maximizing risk because you associate consistency with it is a 'double-edged sword' that leads
to emotionalism, fear, and greed upon a loss.

section_title: THE PERILS OF EXCESSIVE RISK AND TRADING DEVELOPMENT
content:
Applying emotion to your trading leads to havoc; you become reactionary instead of anticipatory.
- The advantageous nature of EMAs on a weekly timeframe helps you stay focused on where
fundamentals are driving price and where momentum is pushing the market.
- This fosters relaxation and patience, allowing you to wait for the market rather than chasing, missing ideal
low-risk, high-probability entry points.
- Precision comes with experience (two decades for ICT); expect a growth period of at least six months to
trust these concepts in real-time.

section_title: PROBABILITIES, INDIVIDUAL RESULTS, AND CONTINUOUS IMPROVEMENT
content:
Trading involves probabilities, not certainties or absolutions. If you're wrong, you will lose money.
- Grossly abusing leverage and risk, regardless of a mentor's experience or results, will lead to significant
losses.
- My results are unique to me, just as yours will be unique to you; we are all uniquely different.
- Don't judge your development against a mentor; judge it against your past self. Consistent effort leads to
improvement, but profitability has its own timing.

section_title: EUR/USD WEEKLY CHART EMA ANALYSIS
content:
Examining the EUR/USD weekly chart with 9 (red) and 18 (blue) Exponential Moving Averages (EMAs).
- When the 9 EMA (red) is above the 18 EMA (blue), and the averages start to stack (opening up and
continually moving higher), it signifies a bullish weekly order flow.
- A crossover of the averages must happen before changing the directional premise. Minor downturns are
not precursors to a crossover.
- This tool filters out uncertainty, helping to maintain focus on the overall bullish movement, even with
bearish weeks or sell-offs.

section_title: INTEGRATING WEEKLY ORDER FLOW WITH INTRADAY ORDER BLOCKS
content:
After identifying a bullish weekly order flow (represented by a shaded area), the focus shifts to a 1-hour
chart within that period.
- The understanding of bullish order blocks (the first bearish candle prior to an aggressive price rally) is
applied.
- When price moves aggressively away from a level (e.g., 1.3325) and then pulls back, we look to buy in the
area of that order block.
- This method is about looking for consistently profitable setups over a long sample size, not cherry-picking
the cleanest major swings.

section_title: IDENTIFYING STRONG SUPPORT AND ACCUMULATION ZONES
content:
Within a bullish weekly order flow, observe intraday order blocks.
- When price drops down into a bearish candle (an order block) and then rallies, and this pattern repeats
with multiple retests of the same level, it indicates accumulation of positions by institutions.
- This answers: 'How do I know that price is going to hold a support level?' When an order block is being
accumulated like this inside of a weekly bullish order flow, you can trust that level as support.
- Why go short when the market is indicating bullishness, even as a day trader? It goes against the grain.

section_title: ANTICIPATING SMART MONEY ACTION AND PRICING
content:
Price behavior inherently repeats. While new traders chase rallies, professional traders identify 'inflated
prices' and wait for better deals by being a buyer.
- The point of origin for a market rally is the bearish candle where 'smart money' was buying, particularly in
the lower third of that candle.
- Price will drop back down into these order blocks. We focus on pure price action, without Fibonacci or
other indicators, to avoid muddled charts and cherry-picking.
- Maintain focus on the larger bullish weekly order flow; intraday declines are opportunities for buying at
wholesale prices.

section_title: CONFIDENCE, DISCIPLINE, AND TRADING HOURS
content:
Routine application of these concepts builds confidence and resilience.
- Losses become minimal, controlled, and at the lower spectrum of risk to your equity.
- This approach dictates specific trading times (e.g., London close), specific days (Tuesday, Wednesday,
Thursday as 'sweet spot'), and focus on one side of the market.
- This unwavering analysis allows you to reach a new level of trading, where losses don't affect you and
you're completely confident and unshaken.

section_title: INTERPRETING DECLINES IN BULLISH ORDER FLOW
content:
Aggressive moves away from a level and the time spent at that level are qualifiers for order blocks.
- During a bullish condition with expected higher prices, when you see prices dropping lower, it's not
indicative of smart money selling.
- In fact, it's the contrary: it's the lack of smart money buying. Retail traders do not move the marketplace.
- These declines are 'red neon signs' signaling that the market is about to go up, and you need to look for
the institutional order block where the next price swing will be anchored.

section_title: CAPITALIZING ON CONSISTENT ORDER BLOCK REACTIONS
content:
Multiple opportunities demonstrate bullish order blocks being respected, leading to aggressive, explosive
price moves of 25-90 pips.
- Catching even one such move a week with proper equity management can build a career.
- Do not be frustrated by missing every move; focus on a small segment of the marketplace that you can
consistently harvest.
- Aggressive drops in price during a bullish order flow are rapid re-pricings to areas where institutional
liquidity (large pockets of willing buyers) rests, leading to quick rallies.

section_title: THE POWER OF INSTITUTIONAL SPONSORSHIP
content:
When you see aggressive moves lower, particularly on a 5-minute chart, it can be scary, leading retail
traders to go short.
- However, these rapid declines are merely price quickly getting down to an area where institutional liquidity
rests, facilitated by large pockets of willing buyers.
- Because institutional interests are vast, price doesn't spend much time there; it takes off and rallies.
- Without institutional sponsorship behind price moves, we have nothing. You must be positioned on the
heels and coattails of these participants, who possess the cash flow and brains to move the marketplace.
